default_annotator.report_text:
targeting specific employees with spear phishing emails advertising fake job advertisements Use of spear phishing emails to attract specific employees with fake job ads.

deploying the More_eggs JScript backdoor malware (aka Terra Loader, SpicyOmelette) Using JavaScript/JScript to execute the More_eggs malware.

This tool, a TTP observed in ITG08 attacks since 2018, is sold on the dark web by an underground malware-as-aservice (MaaS) provider Purchase of malware from a malware-as-a-service provider

the use of Windows Management Instrumentation (WMI) to automate the remote execution of PowerShell scripts Use of Windows Management Instrumentation (WMI) to automate the remote execution of PowerShell scripts

PowerShell commands with base64 encoding, Using PowerShell to execute commands.

PowerShell commands with base64 encoding, Obfuscation of PowerShell commands using base64 encoding.

Metasploit and PowerShell to move laterally and deploy malware. Use of tools such as Metasploit for malware distribution.

the threat actor began by targeting handpicked employees using LinkedIn messaging and email Sending spear phishing emails containing links to malicious payloads.

the threat actor began by targeting handpicked employees using LinkedIn messaging and email Sending spear phishing links via LinkedIn or email.

downloaded a ZIP file containing a malicious Windows Script File (WSF) that initiated the infection routine of the More_eggs backdoor Using Windows scripts to execute malicious commands.

downloaded a ZIP file containing a malicious Windows Script File (WSF) that initiated the infection routine of the More_eggs backdoor Deception of the user to execute a malicious file.

the ZIP file and WSF files were deleted upon a successful malware infection Deletion of malicious files to hide traces.

a nonmalicious decoy document dropped to the disk drive Creation of non-malicious decoy documents to confuse researchers and obfuscate malicious activities.

The spear phishing attacks Sending spear phishing emails containing links to malicious payloads.

The spear phishing attacks Sending spear phishing links via LinkedIn or email.

the installation of the More_eggs JScript backdoor Using JavaScript to execute malicious commands.

established a reverse shell connection to the attacker's command-and-control (C&C) infrastructure Use of web protocols for malicious communications.

established a reverse shell connection to the attacker's command-and-control (C&C) infrastructure Transfer of malicious tools from an external system to the compromised network.

download and execution of files and scripts and running commands using cmd.exe Executing commands through the Windows shell.

download and execution of files and scripts and running commands using cmd.exe Downloading files and scripts.

the More_eggs backdoor later downloaded additional files, including a signed binary shellcode loader and a signed Dynamic Link Library (DLL) Loading of shared modules, such as signed DLLs.

the More_eggs backdoor later downloaded additional files, including a signed binary shellcode loader and a signed Dynamic Link Library (DLL) Transfer of additional malicious files.

to create a reverse shell and connect to a remote host Execution of commands and scripts using PowerShell.

to create a reverse shell and connect to a remote host Communication with the command and control server.

they employed WMI and PowerShell techniques to perform network reconnaissance and move laterally within the environment Using WMI to perform network reconnaissance and lateral movement within the environment.

they employed WMI and PowerShell techniques to perform network reconnaissance and move laterally within the environment Using PowerShell to perform network reconnaissance and lateral movement within the environment.

The attackers used this technique to remotely install a Metasploit reverse TCP stager on select systems, subsequently spawning a Meterpreter session and Mimikatz Using remote access tools such as Meterpreter to establish a session with the compromised system

Meterpreter is a payload component in the Metasploit Framework that uses in-memory DLL injection Meterpreter, a payload component in the Metasploit framework, uses in-memory DLL injection.

Mimikatz is a post-exploitation tool that allows attackers to extract credentials from volatile memory Mimikatz is a post-exploitation tool used to extract credentials from volatile memory

The shellcode loader was observed on one infected device as updater.exe with the Metasploit-style service name APTYnDS1ABEuUHEA, indicating that it was installed as a service the shellcode loader was observed as updater.exe

second stage Meterpreter DLL into memory, allowing the attacker to spawn a Meterpreter session via a handler and initiate the loading of extensions, such as Mimikatz The attacker loaded a second stage of Meterpreter DLL into memory, allowing Meterpreter sessions to be executed and extensions such as Mimikatz to be loaded

the attacker leveraged in-memory attacks by injecting malicious code, in this case Mimikatz, into legitimate system processes The attacker used Metasploit to inject malicious code (Mimikatz) into legitimate system processes.

ITG08 remotely connected to these devices using PowerShell and WMI and downloaded and executed a DLL file, The attacker used PowerShell and WMI to connect and download and execute a DLL file

The process begins with the consistent execution of a malicious DLL using the legitimate regsvr32.exe Windows Utility regsvr32.exe is used

The JScript loader file is highly obfuscated and decodes the More_eggs backdoor in a variety of ways, including RC4 decryption the loader decodes the backdoor

The RC4 key is then used to encrypt data, which is additionally basE91 encoded and sent back to the C&C. BasE91 is a method for encoding binary as ASCII characters encoding methods were used
